title: The Complicated Grief of Inheritance
subtitle: A reading on the overlap between receiving money and mourning the person it came from
excerpt: A reading on the particular complexity of inherited money: that it arrives alongside loss, that its presence is evidence of an absence, and that the usual money-guilt patterns can get tangled with grief in ways that make both harder to process.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on public-domain literature on grief, inheritance, and money psychology.

practice.whenToUse: Read when you've inherited money or received money connected to a loss, and grief and financial discomfort are running together in a way that makes each harder to reach.

content.text: A reading on the specific difficulty of money that comes from loss. Inherited money is different from other unearned windfalls because the person who gave it is gone. The money and the grief arrive together, and they don't separate cleanly.

With any other windfall, you can work through the guilt on its own terms: where did the earning-rule come from, what does keeping the money mean about you, what would you do with it if you let yourself. With inherited money, there is a layer underneath that: the money is here because someone is not. Every time the account balance appears, it is also evidence of the loss.

Some people spend inherited money quickly — not out of recklessness but because having it sit in the account makes the absence more concrete. Others avoid looking at the account for the same reason. Others distribute it generously as a way of staying connected to the person through how the money moves.
When guilt and grief look alike

The guilt that comes with inherited money can be hard to distinguish from grief. Both create a reluctance to engage with the money directly. Both produce an urge to move it on — to charity, to family, to anything that disperses it. Both make holding the money feel like a statement you're not sure you're entitled to make.

The difference is that guilt is about worthiness (do I deserve this?) and grief is about loss (I wish they were here instead). Most people who've inherited money are carrying both at once. Working on one without acknowledging the other tends to stall.

Separating the two strands is useful practical work, not cold analysis. This is grief. This is the earning-rule. They can be named independently. The grief doesn't require money decisions to be resolved. The earning-rule doesn't require the grief to be resolved. Both can be named at the same time without collapsing into each other.

Giving the inherited money a defined period before making decisions about it is a pattern that appears repeatedly in the public-domain literature on inheritance and grief. Not indefinitely, but a defined window in which the money sits without requiring action. This gives both the grief and the guilt their own space, and usually produces clearer decisions at the end of the window.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on public-domain literature on grief, inheritance, and money psychology.
